title: दत्तात्रेय पञ्चरत्नम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री दत्तात्रेय पञ्चरत्नम् ॥

त्रिमूर्तिस्वरूपं गुणातीतरूपं सदानन्दरूपं चिदाकारसौख्यम्।
समाधिस्थितं ज्ञानदीपप्रकाशं भजे दत्तमीशं जगद्वन्द्यपादम्॥ १॥

अनसूययात्रेरपत्यं वरिष्ठं चतुर्वेदशास्त्रार्थतत्त्वप्रबोधम्।
चतुर्विंशतेर्गुरुणां गुरुं तं भजे दत्तमीशं जगद्वन्द्यपादम्॥ २॥

जटाधारिणं दिगम्बरमव्ययं तं गोभिश्चतुर्भिः श्वभिष्षड्भिरेवम्।
परिव्राजकं योगिनामेकनाथं भजे दत्तमीशं जगद्वन्द्यपादम्॥ ३॥

गिरनारवासं सह्याद्रिसंचारिणं शेषशायिनमत्युग्रतापम्।
समस्तापदां नाशकं भक्तवत्सं भजे दत्तमीशं जगद्वन्द्यपादम्॥ ४॥

सदा दत्तदत्तेति सम्बोधयन्तं मुदा भक्तवृन्दैः परिसेव्यमानम्।
कृपापूर्णदृष्ट्या विलोक्य स्वभक्तान् भजे दत्तमीशं जगद्वन्द्यपादम्॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्री दत्तात्रेय पञ्चरत्नम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Dattatreya Pancharatnam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Dattatreya in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Dattatreya festivals and daily worship
description: Dattatreya Pancharatnam — दत्तात्रेय पञ्चरत्नम् — is five gems of devotional verses offered to Lord Dattatreya. Drawn from Dattatreya Tantra, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Devotees recite this prayer to invoke blessings of Dattatreya in all forms and remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. It is also said to grant divine protection and auspiciousness. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Thursday (Guruvar) and during Datta Jayanti and Guru Purnima. Comprising 5 verses, it can be completed in approximately 3 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Dattatreya Pancharatnam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Dattatreya Pancharatnam in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn for Dattatreya devotion and blessings.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Dattatreya Pancharatnam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Dattatreya Pancharatnam (दत्तात्रेय पञ्चरत्नम् — "Five Gems of Dattatreya") is a sacred 5-verse Sanskrit devotional hymn dedicated to Lord Dattatreya — the unique trimurti deity combining Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva — preserved in the Dattatreya Tantra and recited within the Datta Sampradaya tradition of Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh and adjoining regions. As its name pancharatnam (five gems) suggests, the composition is structured as five jewel-like verses, each invoking Datta under his various names (Dattatreya, Avadhuta, Adi-Guru, Smartrigami, Yogiraja) and praying for his grace, his protection from the three-fold suffering, and his guidance on the path of avadhuta-jnana (the wisdom of the supreme renunciate). The phalashruti promises invocation of Datta's blessings in all his forms, removal of sins, and the bestowal of jnana-bhakti-vairagya — knowledge, devotion and dispassion together. (Source: Dattatreya Tantra; Datta Sampradaya tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the term Pancharatnam mean and why are stotras called gems?
seoFaqs.answer: Pancha-ratnam (पञ्चरत्नम्) literally means "five (pancha) gems (ratnas)." In Hindu devotional literature, a particularly concise and beloved short stotra of five verses is often titled Pancharatnam, with each verse considered a precious jewel offered to the deity. The word ratna (gem) signifies that the verse is small but supremely valuable — like a diamond, easy to carry yet of priceless worth. Famous Pancharatnam compositions include: the Vishnu Pancharatnam, the Shiva Pancharatnam, the Ganga Pancharatnam, the Tyagaraja Pancharatna Krithis (the five Carnatic music masterpieces by Saint Tyagaraja), the Guru Pancharatnam, and the Dattatreya Pancharatnam. These five-verse compositions are designed for daily recitation (taking only 5-10 minutes) yet contain the essence of the deity's praise. The Dattatreya Pancharatnam belongs to this Pancharatnam genre and is regarded as one of the gem-compositions in the Datta corpus. (Source: classical Sanskrit stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Dattatreya Pancharatnam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Dattatreya Pancharatnam is most powerfully recited on the following occasions: (1) Daily on Thursday (Guruvar — sacred to Brihaspati and to Datta the Adi-Guru, the supreme day for Guru-worship in Hindu tradition); (2) On Datta Jayanti — Margashirsha Purnima (December full moon) — the principal birthday festival of Lord Dattatreya, the supreme parva of the Datta Sampradaya; (3) On every Purnima (full moon — Datta is considered active especially at full moons) and Pradosham; (4) During the seven-day Datta Saptaha preceding Datta Jayanti, when devotees observe nirjala fast and continuous chanting; (5) On the punyatithis (anniversary days) of the four great Datta-avatars (Shripada Shri Vallabha, Sri Narasimha Saraswati, Manik Prabhu, Sri Swami Samartha); (6) During pilgrimage to Datta-kshetras (Audumbar, Narsobachi Wadi, Ganagapur, Akkalkot, Mahur, Girnar, Pithapuram); (7) Daily morning and evening worship by Datta upasakas at the home shrine; (8) During Guru Purnima (Ashadha Purnima). The hymn's brevity makes daily recitation easy. (Source: Datta Sampradaya tradition; Drikpanchang Datta calendar.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Dattatreya Pancharatnam grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following benefits to regular recitation of the Dattatreya Pancharatnam: (1) Datta-kripa — the direct grace of Lord Dattatreya in all his trimurti forms (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva together); (2) sarva-papa-vinashana — destruction of all sins, since Datta is the Pap-haran-Datta who removes the sins of his devotees; (3) sarva-vighna-nivarana — removal of all obstacles in spiritual and worldly life; (4) jnana-bhakti-vairagya — the simultaneous awakening of jnana (wisdom), bhakti (devotion) and vairagya (dispassion) — the three together being Datta's signature gift since he is the supreme Avadhuta who unites all three; (5) Smartrigami-prasada — Datta's appearance the moment he is remembered; (6) Guru-tattva-anubhuti — direct experience of the Guru-principle, since Datta is the Adi-Guru of all spiritual lineages; (7) protection from evil spirits (bhuta-pishacha-nivarana), as Datta is the great exorcist; (8) graha-shanti — peace from planetary afflictions; (9) the supreme spiritual blessing of avadhuta-jnana — the highest realization beyond all conventional dharma. (Source: Dattatreya Pancharatnam phalashruti; Datta Sampradaya tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of Lord Dattatreya as Adi-Guru?
seoFaqs.answer: Lord Dattatreya is universally venerated in Hindu tradition as the Adi-Guru — the primordial Guru — for several converging reasons. (1) He is the only deity who combines Brahma (the granter of jnana), Vishnu (the granter of bhakti) and Shiva (the granter of vairagya) in a single embodiment — precisely the three qualifications a Guru must transmit to a disciple. (2) The famous Bhagavata Purana (11.7-11.9) records the conversation in which Datta as the Avadhuta tells King Yadu that he has had twenty-four gurus — the earth, air, sky, water, fire, moon, sun, pigeon, python, ocean, moth, bee, elephant, deer, fish, courtesan Pingala, the kurara bird, the child, the maiden, the arrow-maker, the serpent, the spider, the wasp, and the body — teaching that the entire creation is a textbook for the wise. This makes Datta the supreme exemplar of teaching and learning. (3) All major Hindu sampradayas trace their guru-parampara back to Dattatreya — including the Nath Sampradaya, the Mahanubhav, the Datta Sampradaya proper, and many Tantric lineages. (4) He is the patron deity of all sannyasis. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 11.7-11.9; Dattatreya Upanishad.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Dattatreya Pancharatnam differ from the Dattatreya Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are short Sanskrit stotras to Dattatreya recited daily by Datta upasakas, but they differ in length, structure and emphasis. The Dattatreya Dwadash Nama Stotram ("Twelve Names of Dattatreya") consists of essentially a name-litany — invoking Datta under twelve principal names (Dattatreya, Atri-putra, Anasuya-suta, Avadhuta, Yogiraja, Adi-Guru, Smartrigami, Tridev-avatar, Karunakara, Bhakta-vatsala, etc.) — with each name being a meditation on a distinct aspect of Datta's nature. The Dattatreya Pancharatnam ("Five Gems of Dattatreya") consists of five complete stutic verses, each verse being a more developed meditation on Datta's iconographic, mythological or theological aspect, with closing pleas for grace and protection. The Dwadash Nama is more concentrated as a name-power chant; the Pancharatnam is more poetic-stutic. Both are recited together by serious Datta devotees, often along with the Datta Stotram, Datta Bavani (Marathi 52-verse hymn), and the Datta Mahatmya. (Source: Datta Sampradaya stotra-paath corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the iconographic form of Dattatreya invoked in the Pancharatnam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Dattatreya iconographic form invoked in the Pancharatnam — and standard in all Datta worship — is one of the most distinctive in Hindu iconography: (1) Three heads (tri-mukha) — the central Vishnu face with peaceful expression, on the right Brahma with vedas and water-pot, on the left Shiva with matted hair and ash; (2) Six arms (shad-bhuja) — holding the conch (shankha) and discus (chakra) of Vishnu, the rosary (akshamala) and water-pot (kamandalu) of Brahma, and the trident (trishula) and drum (damaru) of Shiva; (3) Four dogs at his feet (chatur-shvana) — representing the four Vedas (Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva) or, in another interpretation, the four yugas; (4) A cow standing behind him — representing Mother Earth (bhumi), the source of dharma and nourishment; (5) The audumbara tree (Ficus racemosa) above him — his eternal sacred abode; (6) Smeared with vibhuti (sacred ash), wearing rudraksha and tulasi, with a peaceful smiling face — the ideal Avadhuta. This is the dhyana-murti meditated upon while reciting the Pancharatnam. (Source: Dattatreya Upanishad; classical Datta iconography.)
